context.project_description: Implement a token tracking hook that instruments LLM calls to measure prompt and completion tokens consumed per query, integrating with the existing instrumentation system.
key_requirements: Extract token usage from provider responses (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens) where available
Fall back to estimation using Tiktoken for providers that don't expose token counts natively
Group metrics by LLM provider and model for cost tracking per query
Integrate with existing InstrumentedProxy and BenchmarkHook systems
constraints: Must leverage existing InstrumentedProxy and hook system without breaking changes
Only extract usage from response metadata when available; no modification of core behavior
Support all major LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) with appropriate fallbacks
Maintain minimal instrumentation overhead for production use
context.existing_context: fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py contains BenchmarkHook protocol and InstrumentedProxy class. fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py defines provider protocols including ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, RerankProvider, VisionProvider with standardized method signatures.
stakeholders: developer tasked with implementing LLM token tracking functionality
system architect responsible for maintaining instrumentation framework
end users who want visibility into LLM query costs

in_scope: implementation of TokenTrackingHook class that extracts token usage from LLM responses
integration with existing InstrumentedProxy and hook system
support for tracking prompt_tokens and completion_tokens per query
export of TokenTrackingHook through standard import paths
out_of_scope: creation of new __init__.py file in fitz_ai/llm/instrumentation/
development of token_counter.py utility module for estimation fallbacks
implementation of full observability dashboard or reporting system
modification of core LLM client behavior or APIs

existing_files: fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Instrumentation system for benchmarking plugin performance
fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — Provider protocols for LLM clients
fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py — OpenAI provider wrappers using the official SDK
fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py — Anthropic provider wrappers using the official SDK
fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py — Cohere provider wrappers using the official SDK
fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py — Ollama provider wrappers using direct HTTP calls
needed_artifacts: fitz_ai/llm/instrumentation/token_tracker.py — Implements BenchmarkHook subclass to extract and track token consumption per LLM call
fitz_ai/llm/instrumentation/__init__.py — Re-exports TokenTrackingHook for clean import paths

assumptions.assumption: Providers return token info in responses
assumptions.impact: Would require alternative logic like Tiktoken or estimation

assumptions.assumption: We can plug into `InstrumentedProxy` and hooks system
assumptions.impact: May require custom wrapping approach instead

assumptions.assumption: Embedding and vision APIs consume tokens
assumptions.impact: Need to expand logic if Ollama doesn't track or tracks differently

assumptions.assumption: All major LLMs expose token consumption in response metadata (where applicable)
assumptions.impact: Might require estimation or custom parsing if missing

approaches.name: Approach 1: Direct Hook Integration with Response Parsing
approaches.description: Builds a dedicated `TokenTrackingHook` that directly intercepts LLM calls and parses response metadata for tokens.
pros: Aligns with existing architecture using established instrumentation system
Non-breaking - does not modify provider interfaces or methods
Scalable - can easily support new providers by extending response parsing logic
Minimal overhead - hook-based approach is lightweight and production-ready
cons: Failure mode: If providers don't consistently return metadata, fallback estimation can be inaccurate or miss tokens entirely
best_for: Production systems requiring cost tracking without breaking existing contracts

approaches.name: Approach 2: Decorator-based Token Tracking
approaches.description: Wraps provider methods directly using decorators that track before/after calls to add token metadata.
pros: More granular control over exactly what is tracked
cons: Requires modifying existing providers, violating non-breaking constraint
Creates tight coupling between tracking and core logic
High operational burden - would require changes to all provider implementations
best_for: Systems where modification of core behavior is acceptable and fine-grained control is needed

architecture.recommended: Approach 1: Direct Hook Integration with Response Parsing
architecture.reasoning: Approach 1 is correct because it aligns with existing architecture by using the established instrumentation system without modifying core behavior. It's non-breaking since it doesn't change provider interfaces or methods, only adds tracking capability on top of existing hooks. It's scalable and can easily support new providers by extending response parsing logic while maintaining architectural purity. Approach 2 would break existing provider contracts which violates constraints.

key_tradeoffs.Hook-based vs Decorator-based approach: Hook-based approach provides non-breaking integration with existing instrumentation system, while decorator approach requires modifying provider implementations and creates tight coupling.
key_tradeoffs.Direct parsing vs Fallback estimation: Direct parsing from response metadata is more accurate but fails when providers don't expose tokens; fallback to Tiktoken estimation ensures tracking works at cost of accuracy.

technology_considerations: Tiktoken for token estimation - uses existing codebase dependency
OpenAI response structure parsing - handles usage field in responses
Anthropic response structure parsing - handles usage fields with input_tokens/output_tokens
Cohere response structure parsing - handles meta.tokens field extraction
Ollama response structure parsing - handles prompt_eval_count/eval_count fields
Regular expressions for string-based token pattern matching
architecture.scope_statement: This task produces two files: `fitz_ai/llm/instrumentation/token_tracker.py` and `fitz_ai/llm/instrumentation/__init__.py`. The challenge is correctly parsing response structures from multiple providers to extract token usage, with appropriate fallbacks.

adrs.title: ADR: Token Usage Tracking via Benchmark Hook
adrs.context: Implement token usage tracking for LLM calls to enable cost monitoring and optimization. The system needs to extract prompt_tokens and completion_tokens from provider responses when available, with fallback estimation using Tiktoken.
adrs.decision: Create a dedicated TokenTrackingHook class that implements the BenchmarkHook protocol to intercept LLM calls at the instrumentation layer without modifying existing provider interfaces or behavior
adrs.rationale: This approach aligns with existing architecture patterns (instrumentation system), is non-breaking to providers, scalable for new providers through response parsing extensions, and follows the 'instrumentation without modification' principle established elsewhere in the codebase. It integrates cleanly with existing hook infrastructure via register_hook() calls.
consequences: Minimal operational overhead - only requires adding one hook registration call
Direct integration with existing instrumentation system maintains architectural purity
Separation of concerns between provider logic and tracking capabilities
Fallback estimation using Tiktoken ensures tracking works even when native metrics aren't provided
alternatives_considered: Decorator-based approach - rejected because it would require modifying all provider implementations, violating the non-breaking constraint
Proxy-based extension of existing proxy system - rejected as overly complex for this specific task and duplicating functionality already present in BenchmarkHook protocol

adrs.title: ADR: Token Usage Data Exclusion from Provider Response
adrs.context: The TokenTrackingHook approach excludes detailed token usage data from provider responses to maintain architectural purity and avoid breaking existing provider contracts
adrs.decision: Only basic response metadata will be tracked, not detailed token counting information that would require modifying provider interfaces
adrs.rationale: This maintains backward compatibility with existing providers while keeping the hook architecture clean. The tradeoff is that full token usage analytics must be computed separately through post-processing rather than being available inline with responses.
consequences: Token analysis requires additional processing layer or batch computation instead of real-time response enrichment
Potential performance overhead from separate token counting systems that may not align well with provider throughput
Limited ability to provide immediate feedback about usage patterns during request processing
alternatives_considered: Modify provider interfaces to include token counts directly in responses - would break contracts and increase complexity
Implement a hybrid approach where some providers support inline token data while others require post-processing - increases maintenance burden and inconsistency

components.name: TokenTrackingHook
components.purpose: A benchmark hook that tracks token usage from LLM calls for cost tracking purposes
responsibilities: Extract prompt_tokens and completion_tokens from provider responses when available
Fall back to estimation using Tiktoken when native metrics aren't provided
Group metrics by LLM provider and model for cost tracking
interfaces: on_call_start(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> Any
on_call_end(context: Any, result: Any, error: Exception | None) -> None

integration_points: ExternalSystem — TokenTrackingHook integrates with the existing instrumentation system via BenchmarkHook protocol, registering itself with register_hook() function in fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source

phases.name: Instrument Provider Interfaces
phases.objective: Add token usage tracking to LLM chat providers by leveraging existing instrumentation system
key_risks: Token extraction varies by provider implementation

phases.name: Implement Token Extraction Logic
phases.objective: Extract token usage information from responses for each LLM provider
deliverables: Provider-specific token extraction functions for OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama
Unified interface for accessing token counts
key_risks: Missing token metadata in some responses

phases.name: Integrate with LLM Client Factory
phases.objective: Automatically instrument all chat providers during initialization
deliverables: Modified LLM client factory to wrap ChatProvider instances
Configuration option for enabling/disabling token tracking
key_risks: Breaking changes to existing provider interfaces

risks.description: Different LLM providers return token usage in varying formats and locations within their responses, requiring provider-specific parsing logic that may break if response structures change.
risks.mitigation: Implement robust fallback mechanisms with comprehensive error handling for each provider's token extraction logic to prevent failures when response schemas vary or are updated by providers.
risks.contingency: If parsing fails, default to logging 'unknown' or 0 tokens and alert monitoring system to investigate the specific provider response format change.
risks.verification: Verify that token usage extraction works correctly for OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, and Ollama providers by creating unit tests with mock responses from each provider.

risks.description: Instrumentation system may introduce performance overhead or race conditions when tracking token counts across concurrent queries.
risks.mitigation: Use thread-safe data structures for storing token usage and minimize instrumentation overhead by only instrumenting necessary methods (chat, chat_stream).
risks.contingency: If performance degradation is observed, add sampling logic to track only a percentage of queries or disable tracking in production environments.
risks.verification: Run load tests with instrumentation enabled to confirm no significant impact on query processing throughput.

risks.description: LLM provider API changes could break token extraction logic if they alter response schemas or remove usage metadata fields.
risks.mitigation: Implement versioned parsing logic and monitor provider release notes for schema changes that might affect token reporting.
risks.contingency: Establish a process to quickly update token extraction methods upon provider API breaking changes, with rollback capability to previous working versions.
risks.verification: Verify parsing resilience by simulating schema modifications in test environments and confirm graceful degradation when fields are missing.

recommended_contingencies: Implement comprehensive fallback logic that defaults to tracking token usage as 'unknown' or 0 if provider-specific extraction fails
Create a monitoring alert for any failures in token parsing across different LLM providers during query execution
